front: What is a cache in the context of parallel computing?
back: A cache is a small, fast memory located close to a processor core that stores copies of frequently accessed data from main memory. Its purpose is to speed up data access and reduce the latency of memory operations.

In a shared-memory multiprocessor system, the cache coherence problem arises when multiple caches hold copies of the same memory location. If one processor modifies its copy, the other copies become stale, leading to inconsistent views of data.

A write-through policy is a method for maintaining cache coherence where every write operation to the cache is immediately written through to the main memory. This ensures memory is always up-to-date but can create a memory bottleneck.

False sharing is a performance-degrading phenomenon that occurs when two or more processors frequently write to different variables that reside on the same cache line. This causes the cache line to be invalidated and shuttled between caches, even though the processors are not actually accessing the same data.

front: What is the MESI protocol? Name the four states.
back: The MESI protocol is a widely used cache coherence protocol. It defines four states for a cache line: Modified (exclusive dirty), Exclusive (exclusive clean), Shared (read-only), and Invalid (stale or not present).
